segment-text: Pretty, 30ish JOY JORDAN and teary-eyed STUART sit opposite each other. He is trying to resist bursting into tears as they finish dessert.
Good. Well. I had a really nice time.
Of course, you know I've always had a nice time with you.
'Cause you know I could never hate you. At the same time, I just don't think I could ever...you know... you. In the way you should be...
Well. The food here was excellent- I'm gonna recommend it to my sisters! How many stars did it get?
And the dam cracks wide open. He bawls. A pause.
Do you feel better now? (STUART nods) Me too.
It's really good we had this talk.
Before things went too far... You know, got too serious.
Yeah. I'm sorry. I'm too serious.
The thing is, I want to do what's right - for both of us. I spent a lot of time...examining...my heart. And I felt that you deserved my honesty.
Now I just want to make you whole again.
...'Cause I was afraid we wouldn't be able to be friends anymore.
Oh, but you know how it is. And well, most guys...
I know. If only most guys were like you.
But then I'd be like most guys.
(laughs) Oh, Stuart. If only I felt the way I'd like to feel with you... Life is so unfair. It's all my fault.
I know. (a beat) Are you sure...?
I want to show you something I got for you.
(hands her a gift) Open it up.
(discovers a pewter ashtray) Oh, but Stuart. This is...oh, this is beautiful.
Thanks. It's a Gainsevoort reproduction. Boston, late 1800's. I sent away for it just after we had our...first date.
Oh, I just love it. It's a...it's a collector's item.
(laughs) It almost makes me want to start smoking again!
Oh, Stuart. Thank you. This really means something to me. I'll always treasure it...as a token...

No, you won't. (retrieves his gift; a sudden shift in emotion:) 'Cause this is for the girl who loves me. The girl who cares for me, for who I am, not what I look like. I wanted you to know what you'd be missing. You think I don't appreciate art. You think I don't understand fashion. You think I'm not hip. You think I'm pathetic, a nerd, a lard-ass fatso. You think I'm shit. Well, you're wrong. 'Cause I'm champagne. And you're shit. And till the day you die, you, not me, will always be shit.
Thunder. Rain. STUART's car drives by.
JOY sits beside STUART, who is driving. A plastic "World of Pewter" shopping bag hangs by the glove compartment.
JOY gets out. STUART drives away.
ALLEN talks to his psychiatrist, BILL MAPLEWOOD.
...I dunno, but whenever I see her I just want to...you know...I want to undress her, I want to tie her up and pump her pump pump pump till
she screams bloody murder. And then
dick shoots right through her and
at each other... But I don't know
HELEN parks, gets out of her car, walks towards the
apartment building entrance. 8. INT. HIGH RISE LOBBY - EVENING. 8.
HELEN walks towards the elevator and waits beside ALLEN. ALLEN mumbles something. HELEN is oblivious.
ALLEN and HELEN step inside. They ride in silence up to their floor.
ALLEN and HELEN emerge from the elevator.
And they walk their separate ways.

ALLEN drops his stuff. He takes a shower. He lies down. He glances at a book of poetry. CLOSE ON the jacket photo of the author and accompanying brief bio (i.e. "Nominated for the PEN/Faulkner Prize for poetry, Helen Jordan has taught at Harvard, Yale...") He leans his head against the wall, listens to HELEN making love to somebody next door: "Oh, Helen! Helen! Helen!" etc. He removes his ear from the wall, sits on the bed, a phone book beside him. He starts flipping randomly through the phone book. He turns to a page. He dials a number from the book. No answer. He sighs. Tries to next number down. A man picks up. ALLEN hangs up immediately. He dials again. A beautiful-sounding WOMAN picks up.
ALLEN hangs up. He buries his head in his hands.

JOY sits in the kitchen with her sister TRISH, who is doing her nails. Trish's baby CHLOE lies in JOY'S lap. JUANITA the cleaning woman cleans up in the b.g. The family dog KOOKI teethes on a bone. Cute little TIMMY appears, in a Robocop-style outfit.
(aiming a laser at JOY) Die, Aunt Joy! Die!
She tries to go after him, but he has already run off.
Oh, leave him alone, Trish. He's just going through a phase.
(trying to make light) It's okay. I'm strong enough.
Oh, Joy, Joy. What's the matter?
I don't know what it is, but I feel there's so much hostility directed at me.
It's okay. Because now maybe you'll listen to me.
You've got to eat red meat.
Oh, I knew that's how you'd react, but it's true. I've been watching you and, well... My doctor says just once a month...
Really. It's the best thing for the skin. It'll clear it all up.
It's fine now, but in another few years... Please, Joy. I'm speaking for your own good.
A tender moment: TRISH and JOY hold hands.
Being around you and the kids...
Oh... And I'm so happy you're happy. 'Cause all this time I've been thinking you were so miserable.
Oh, Trish! That's too funny, when I couldn't be happier.
It's just, what with your music career never really...
Oh, I know, it will be! I just know it! And then you'll move out of Mom and Dad's...
Oh, I will. Already I feel I'm off to a fresh start!
That's right. Just because you've hit 30, doesn't mean you can't be fresh anymore.

You know, Joy, I've never told you this before, but now that we're older, and I feel so bonded to you, well...the truth is - oh, I know this sounds horrible, but I feel I have to be fully open with you, get beyond all the old barriers, sibling nonsense - well, the truth is I always thought you would never amount to much. That you'd end up alone, without a career or anything. Really, it's what we all thought. Mom, Dad, Helen...everyone... I'd always prayed we'd all be wrong, but you had always seemed to...doomed to failure. But now I see, it's not true. There is a glimmer of hope for you after all. Oh, I know I'm repeating myself, but, oh... (tears well up) I'm so happy for you...

It is sunny and warm. Couples straight and gay walk hand in hand, families picnic, beautiful people sunbathe. BILL observes the pleasant tranquillity from atop a hill. Suddenly he pulls out a machine gun and starts shooting at everyone. Bloodshed everywhere. Then silence.
BILL sits opposed his PSYCHIATRIST, to whom the VOICE belongs.
I don't kill myself at the end.
Do you see this as something positive?
How do you feel at the end?
Much better. I wake up happy. Feeling good. But then I get very depressed because I'm living in reality.
And you wouldn't kill people in real life.
My patients are ugly. Their problems are trite. Each one thinks he is unique. On a professional level they bore me. On a personal level I have no sympathy. They deserve what they get.
No. But she's not too interested, either. So really there's no problem there, when you think about it, on a certain level.

Reminiscent of BILL'S dream: schoolchildren are playing, teachers supervising. A laborer is eating lunch with a co-worker. A little boy is sitting alone in the shade of a tree. BILL pulls up to the side of the field and observes from his parking space. He gets back in his car.
BILL is at the wheel again, a little tense.
BILL pulls into the lot. He walks into the 7-11.
BILL picks up a Boy's Life, pays for it.

BILL gets into the back seat of his car and sets to masturbating. SHOPPERS walk by with shopping carts, little children, oblivious to the activity inside Bill's car. Finally, BILL gets out of his car, tucks in his shirt, tosses the scrunched-up magazine into a dumpster, returns to his car and pulls out.
BILL comes home as Trish finishes preparing the dinner table. He gives her a peck on the cheek. Their kids are eating in the adjacent TV Room.
So how was work today, Hon?
KOOKI calms down somewhat. BILLY ENTERS, opens the refrigerator, pours himself a soda. He looks dejected.
He's (making quotation marks with her fingers) "depressed."
I don't wanna talk about it.
And he leaves for the TV room again.
Ignore him. He's just doing it for attention. He thinks you'll be impressed. As if. So anyway... Joy came by today.
Oh, God, I dunno, and frankly... I'm concerned. I mean, she's not like me. She doesn't "have it all." She pretends to be happy, but I can see right through her:
Why do you think that is?

To be frank, I think she's lazy. She's not a go-getter, like me or Helen. And she's so picky. I gave Damien Ross her phone number, for what it's worth, and Joy sounded interested, naturally, but...I dunno. I'm afraid to have to say it, but truly it's what I believe: she'll always be alone.
Oh, Bill. Sometimes I wonder how any of your patients can talk to you.
Sometimes I wonder if they'll ever stop. I should tape some for you.
Would you? Would you really? So that I could listen, too?
You're such a tease. You know I wouldn't tell anyone.
Well, maybe not as secretive as you.
What secret would you like me to tell you?
IRISH puts her arms around BILL.
(whispers coyly) Like how come no matter how much you treat me like shit, I can't help loving you even more?
While TRISH puts CHLOE and TIMMY to bed, BILLY comes and sits beside BILL on his bed.
Well, you know how sometimes your penis gets erect...well, sometimes
it gets so excited that a sticky milky substance shoots out.
Yes, only come can be used as a verb as well. (a beat) Billy?
Now Billy, it's alright if you haven't.
But everyone else in class had and... I want to come, too!
Awn, now, it's okay, it's okay. Have you tried playing with yourself?
I dunno. I don't know what to do.
Do you want me to...show you?
BILLY buries his head in BILL'S lap.
Aw, Billy. Don't worry. You're normal. You'll come. One day. You'll see.
JOY is preparing to cook a steak. She is wearing rubber globes. HELEN sits with a coffee mug at the kitchen table.
Y'know, people were always putting New Jersey down. None of my friends can believe I live here. But that's because they don't get it: I'm living in a state of irony.
Are you sure you don't wanna stay and have dinner with me?
I can't. I'm giving another reading/ book-signing over at Barnes & Noble, and then Jamal is taking me out - although I promised Fabiacho...Uch. Everybody wants me...
Yeah!... So, uh, how are you doing?
Oh, fine. Could you hold a second? Helen?
HELEN is almost out the door.
(into phone) Sorry. That was my sister leaving.
So, um, Trish told me you might be calling.

Oh, I know how weird these things can be, but I've always had such faith in Trish's judgment that I thought why not. It's not like I've got some huge social life. I mean, I do have a social life. It's just not huge.
Oh, really? That's so nice to hear. Most people seem so confident...
Or, well, you know... They're just real jerks.
Oh, I'm sorry, I'm just trying to thaw this steak...but it's so hard and... I'm sorry.
Oh, no. No. Don't stop. Not because of me.
Oh, but I feel I'm being so rude.
Oh, yeah. Don't worry. Nobody's listening in. God, you're just like me.
You mean, when we go out? Well, where do you want to go? I'm pretty easy to please. I hate getting all dressed up.
Oh, just a pair of jeans. Why?
Not too tight. They fit okay. But why do you...?
I don't mean the jeans. I mean underneath. What are you wearing underneath? Check.
(starts looking inside her jeans) Underneath? Well, but Damien, underneath is just... (pauses, suddenly alarmed) This isn't Damien...is it?
Are you getting wet? Is your pussy all--?

VOICES of neighborhood children can be heard from outside the window. ALLEN (the man behind the VOICE that was just on the phone with JOY) rises to the sitting position and puts a bookmark in the phonebook. He notices the mess he made on the wallpaper. The stain looks permanent. He covers it up with a postcard. (There are many other postcards already thumbtacked to the wall.) The doorbell rings. ALLEN rises.
ALLEN opens the door, sees KRISTINA. She is very big and very overweight.
Did you hear what happened to Pedro?
He was found bludgeoned to death in his apartment this morning.
Yeah. And supposedly his penis is missing.
Yeah, well, Carla in 2B is collecting money for the funeral, if you feel like it. Apparently he had no family, no known friends...Gee, if I'da know... I mean, I did always say hi, I think.
Me too, if it's the guy I'm thinking of.
By the way, um, I've got an extra ticket to the play-offs tonight. Wanna come with me?
Nah. Thanks. I got too much work.
Oh. (a beat) Well, anyway, I just thought I'd tell you about Pedro.
KRISTINA stands outside ALLEN'S door and stares at it dejectedly. Finally, tearing up the ticket, she walks back to her apartment, shutting the door behind her. 26. EXT. FLORIDA/ARIZONA 26.
LENNY and MONA, in their 60s, sit at the kitchen table. There are dishes shattered on the floor.
Good. (a beat) I'm gonna clean up.
MONA rises, walks to her bedroom, lies down. LENNY clears the table.
And LENNY starts vacuuming up the debris on the floor. MONA goes to the bathroom, opens the medicine cabinet.
She has found a good enough valium substitute, but there are only two pills left in the bottle. She swallows them with a glass of water, lies down again. The phone rings.
Oh, I'm fine. How are you?
Did you watch Leno last night?... Mom?
(she bursts into tears) He's leaving me! Your father's leaving me!
Mom, what are you talking about?
Can you keep this secret? Top secret?
Yes, yes, of course, Mom, but -
He says...he doesn't love me anymore.
Mom, I'm sure he doesn't mean it.
Well, he does fucking mean it! He wants a divorce!
You don't believe me? Talk to him! (calling) Lenny!
It's Trish! She wants to talk to you!
Is it true what Mom said?
I never used that word. (calling) Mona! What are you telling the kids?
MONA comes wobbling into the room.
(to TRISH) She'll call you back. (hanging up; to MONA) Did I use the word divorce?
You said you didn't want to live with me anymore!
Answer my question: did I use the word divorce?
You said you didn't love me anymore!
Good. I just want that much clear. Now sit down now next to me.
Leave it. The machine'll get it. It's probably Joy.
(on the machine) Hi, Mom. Hi, Dad. It's Joy. Just called to say hi, but I guess you're out having a good time. Oh, well. I guess I'll talk to you tomorrow then. Okay. Bye.
I dunno. I just want to be alone.
But I can let you be alone more, if that's what you want.
Look: Things change... People change... Whatever. I want out.
You're in love with Diane Freed.
Well, you're in love with someone. Someone younger, probably.
Lenny, it's okay. I'm not dumb. These things happen. I'll get over it. I just wish you had done this twenty years ago. Now I'll have to get another fucking face-lift.
I'm in love with no one else.
The CAMERA DOLLIES across a maze of carrels, finally landing on JOY, at work as a telephone sales operator.
(on phone) Hi! Is this Mrs. Mammangakis?... Hi! I'm calling from UniCard of America because you've been such a good customer that--.
JOY dials the number of the next name on the list, and the next. Everyone hangs up on her. Until:
ALLEN is lying in bed. The phone rings. He picks it up.
Hi! I'm calling from UniCard of America because you've been such a good-.
I'm sorry, I don't think so. But perhaps you're familiar with out--.

ALLEN hangs up. But then, for a moment, he ponders the familiarity of her voice. Ditto JOY. She decides to dial him again. ALLEN answers, but says nothing. They both hang on, listening to each other's silence, then hang up.
JOY can be heard singing and playing her guitar.
JOY, sitting on her bed, finishes her melancholy Joni Mitchell-like song. A display of macrame objects d'art adorns the wall. The phone rings. She answers it.
Hello. This is Detective Berman from the County Police Department. I'd like to speak with a Ms. Joy Jordan?

POLICEMEN, a CORONER, a SUPER, ETC. AL. Busy themselves while STUART's body is being removed. BERMAN holds Stuart's suicide note ("Dear Joy, I can't live without you. Love, Stuart. P.S. The ashtray is yours.") in a baggie in his hand.
I'm sorry to disturb you, Ms. Jordan, but I'm afraid I have some bad news for you...
A bright, cheerful day. The parking lot is full.
JOY looks over at a corner desk where a woman is working the phones and starts crying. Her neighbor, NANCY, hearing the sniffling, pauses in the midst of her work.
You know, the guy who used to sit over at the corner there?
You mean where Pam is sitting?
Was he kind of tall and a little hunched?
Oh, Joy. I'm not sure. Did he work here long?
NANCY calls across to another neighbor, KAY.
Kay, do you remember a guy named Stuart who used to work here over where Pam is now?
Huh. Now which one was he?
I'm not sure. May be Tom knows. Tom?
Co-worker TOM, who has been listening in, swivels over from the other side.
Sorry, Nancy, I don't. I mean, I do vaguely remember some guy who worked over there - I think... but I'm not really sure.
Yeah, how did he die, Joy?
JOY's phone rings. She picks up.
This is Stuart's mother. (a beat) I hope you fucking rot in hell.
Hey, Joy. Was he kind of Latino- looking and a little acne-scarred?
Yeah, you know, like Edward James Olmos?
NANCY, KAY, and TOM continue discussing and disputing STUART's identity.
(picking at her food) Uch. I don't know why I suggested this place. Joy recommended it...
Well, at least we're together. I never get to see you, you're so busy.
In fact, if I have to do one more interview...
I guess it's hard, all this success.

It's just I'm so tired of...of being admired all the time. All these men... I mean, they're all beautiful, artistic minds, great sex, the whole package...but hollow, you know what I mean? I feel nobody's really honest with me. Nobody wants me for me.
Oh, Trish. If only I had your husband, kids, carpool.
Well, I may "have it all," but sometimes I wonder what my life would have been like if I'd actually tried to write a novel.
Oh, I'm sure it would have been good.
No, I don't need that kind of success.
Uch, listen to us. We who have everything while Joy... What does she have?
You're right. And she's just getting older.
Last night she called - I was in bed with Vilmos - no, Huraki - and she was in tears. She told me she'd quit her job...
She said she wanted to "change" her life. Do "good" work with the poor, the needy...
Don't even try. She understand she already is good. She doesn't need to do good.
And what about her music career?
I don't know, but don't hold your breath. Anyway, listen. This is all top secret. She doesn't want anyone to know.
Oh. (a beat) But she told you.
She felt she could trust me.
'Cause I always thought I was someone she could confide in.
A BUSBOY comes by, refills their water glasses, leaves.
Oh. I-I thought it was top secret.
Just for Joy. Sensitive Joy. Everyone else knows.
Can I help you with anything else?
The WAITER leaves the check, clears the plates, goes off. TRISH picks up the check, looks at it.
Thanks for lunch. I really enjoyed this.
A little league gave is in progress. 11-year-old JOHNNY is at bat. There is a hush. Bases are loaded. BILLY encourages him from the sidelines
JOE, JOHNNY's father and the team's coach, mutters to himself, tense.
BILL observes JOHNNY's TEAMMATES muttering to themselves.
I can't believe he's up at bat now.
I swear I'm not playing Little League next year if he's on the team.
RONALD FARBER, the biggest and perhaps dumbest of the teammates, joins in.
JOHNNY concentrates. The pitcher throws the ball and...STRIKE!
My kid is a fucking spaz.
Do we have to stay and watch this?
Concentrate, Johnny! You can do it!
You hit the fucking ball or I'm gonna smash your fucking face in.
(to BILL) You know, this really is unfair to the other kids.
While BILLY and JOHNNY play video games, BILL and JOE eat at a table.
Bill, I dunno. Maybe I should talk to you. You're a specialist in these things, aren't you?
Well, I don't know. That depends...
Look, my son's a fag. I'm not blind to these things.
How come you're so sure he's...gay?
Well, it's just sometimes...appearances can be deceiving. And besides. Even if you're right. There's not much you can do, is there?
What do you think would happen if I got him a professional...you know...
Hooker. You know, the kind that can teach things...first-timers, you know...break him in.
You're right, you're right. It's too late. He is...what he is. Forget I said anything.
Maybe you ought to discuss it with Betty.
I can't. I can't talk to her.
Things aren't too good between us...
Dad, can Johnny sleep over tonight?
Well, that's up to Joe here.
JOHNNY and BILLY hold each other as they jump for joy.
The lights are on. The TV is on. The family can be heard laughing and playing some board game.
BILL is preparing hot fudge sundaes. He mixes some powdered drugs into the fudge before pouring it onto the ice cream. He brings a tray loaded with bowls of sundaes into the TV room.
Here, take. You only live once.
Everyone grabs a bowl, except JOHNNY.
Well, is there something you'd like instead?
Oh, leave him alone. He's fine.
No, but there must be something...
Do you have any grape Hi C?
I could go pick some up.
Bill, don't be silly. He doesn't need anything. It's late.
Do you have any tuna salad?
Look at that Timmy, can hardly keep his eyes open. Come on, Timmy. (to BILLY and JOHNNY) You boys, don't stay up to late!

TRISH shuffles off with little CHLOE and TIMMY. BILL returns to the kitchen, hastily fixes a tuna sandwich, spiking it with gobs of his powder. BILL brings the sandwich in to JOHNNY. He finds BILLY already fallen asleep.
I don't know. I guess he just conked out.
JOHNNY is glued to the TV. He doesn't touch the tuna sandwich.
Aren't you going to eat the sandwich?
Dr. Maplewood, would it be alright if I ate this tomorrow?
(not losing his self-control) Well, sure, but I don't know if it's taste any good tomorrow.
JOHNNY examines the sandwich. He turns back to the TV. Just when all seems lost, however, he takes a bite.
BILL then rises, checks in on TRISH, CHLOE, and TIMMY: they are all sound asleep. BILL pauses in the hallway to look at a family portrait. Then he returns to the TV room.

But JOHNNY too is now sound asleep. BILL takes away the unfinished tuna sandwich and plate and brings them into the kitchen. He dumps the tuna remains into the garbage, puts the plate into the dishwasher, turns it on. BILL lifts BILLY up and carries him off to bed. He tucks him in. BILL returns to the TV room, where JOHNNY lies. He places him on the couch, then stands hovering over him. The TV is left on.
The gardener is mowing the lawn. A postman drops off the mail. Little children play ball.
TIMMY, BILLY, and JOHNNY are in the TV room watching TV. TRISH snuggles in bed next to BILL. They are very kissy-kissy.
Oh, Honey. I feel so good now.
I haven't slept so well in so long.
It's weird. I feel as if we... Bill, did we? I mean, did you... Did I...?
Okay. (a beat) It's funny, 'cause I remember I was dreaming and you were there... and Billy...and Timmy and Chloe... and Johnny Grasso...
I can't really remember anything more, except...I don't know. Oh, Bill. Please don't get mad at me. I know you hate it when I ask, but... Do you still?
Oh, Bill, and I do too! I'm sorry I need to keep being reminded, it's just...
I know. And it's my fault.
Just when things get hot and heavy, CHLOE starts screaming o.s. Pause.
She rises and leaves the room.
JOHNNY sits at the table with the funnies. BILL is sipping coffee, reading the paper. TIMMY runs around in the b.g.
How come you're not playing together?
I don't know. Billy just said he didn't feel like it. (a beat) Dr. Maplewood?
Can you drive me home now?
Well, sure, but...are you having a good time?
He throws up on the funnies.
'Cause I really had a good time.
Good. Billy did too. We all did.
BILL puts his arm around him affectionately. JOHNNY, very sleepy, leans in close against him.
And he falls asleep, his head sinking into BILL'S lap. A string of saliva drips from JOHNNY's lips onto BILL's pants.
The lights are on. The TV is on.
45. INT. MAPLEWOOD HOME - TV ROOM - LATER. 45.
BILLY joins BILL on the sofa. KOOKI is humping a chair across from them.
Did you have a good time with Johnny?
It was okay. He's a little girlish though.
Dad. Do you know how many inches your penis is?
Ronald Farber said his penis is twelve inches long. Do you think that's possible?
What Ronald Farber doesn't know is that it's not length, but width that matters.
...Yeah. But it's no use. Nothing comes.
You have to be patient. Your friend Ronald Farber, I can assure you, is full of crap.
Yeah. I bet yours is a lot wider and longer.
Do you want me to measure?
BILLY smiles, leans affectionately against his father.
Through the glass door a group of newly-arrived immigrants can be seen walking by. SUPERVISOR looks JOY over.
You've come at a god time. The place is in turmoil now. Everyone's preparing to strike.
W-why are they going to strike?

I can't tell you the half of it: the deceit, the corruption. The union wants to close down the school. They don't care about these poor immigrants. It's heartbreaking. Now I believe in unions, I'm sensitive to their needs, I cried at "The Grapes of Wrath", I sing to Woody Guthrie. But that was another time, another place. Today unions are just a new kind of corporation. It's tragic, but believe me, those teachers making sacrifices for the union, well, the union's not going to make any sacrifices for them. Those union leaders, they go home with pay-checks, the teachers end up with nothing. Now we need people who can be dedicated, who aren't in it just for the money. Because this job takes courage. Do you know what some of these refugees have endured? Concentration camps. And now their dream of coming to America is realized, and this union doesn't care. It's all about greed. Selfishness. Now Joy, can I count on you?
O-of course...I want to do the right, um, thing...I mean, I believe in, um, you know...being counted on...
Do you know what these strikers are? Spoiled brats. Trust-funded artistes. They don't know the meaning of work, of giving... But just remember, if you hear the word "benefits," don't listen.
PICKETERS march along the sidewalk. JOY approaches and hesitates.
(chanting) Don't cross the line! Don't cross the line!
You know, there are some people in real need in there.
I'm sorry. I think you're making a terrible mistake.
As she crosses the picket line they shout epithets at her. Someone throws an egg at her. It cracks against her head. She runs inside.
Strangers look askance at JOY. When the elevator arrives at her floor she gets out, relieved.
49. INT. ESL SCHOOL TEACHERS LOUNGE - DAY. 49.

Administrators welcome JOY, handing out general information. The air is festive. JOY finds the other TEACHERS cleaning tomatoes and egg off their clothes. RHONDA, a teacher, pulls her aside towards the window from which the strikers can be seen.
It's so sad. I mean, it really is pathetic. Such losers. Really, I feel sorry for them.
There is a slight commotion across the room. TEACHER #1 is upset, crying a little. TEACHER #2 comforts her.
You're not a scab. You're a strike-breaker.
JOY enters. The STUDENTS do not have welcoming faces.
Good morning. My name is Joy Jordan.
She writes her name on the blackboard.
I am your new teacher. Now.
JOY is about to do roll-call when:
The STUDENTS start changing, "We want Marsha!" One student, however, does not join in. He shouts at his classmates:
...I'm not a scab. I'm a strike-breaker.

Intensity grips HELEN as she works on a poem at her desk. She rises, as if in pain, and starts pacing. Strange sounds emanate from her mouth. She pounds a fist against her head. Finally she leaps into bed, thrashing about as if possessed.
I'm no good! I'm no good! I am nothing! Nothing! Zero!...
ALLEN is on the other end of the line. His breathing is heavy, low, constrained. He is sweating.

(voice disguised) I know who you are and you are nothing. You think you are fucking something, but you are fucking nothing. You are empty. You are zero. You are a black hole, and I am going to fuck you so bad you're gonna be coming out of your ears.
